skill.name: bitbucket-devops
skill.description: Automates Bitbucket Cloud pipeline, pull request, repository, and log workflows through local Node.js helper commands and Bitbucket API calls.
skill.summary: Use Bitbucket Cloud helpers to inspect pipelines, collect failed logs, trigger builds, and support CI debugging from an AI coding session.
skill.author: Apra-Labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits from documentation and bundled Bitbucket API specifications; those OpenAPI and Markdown examples are mostly false positives. Real risk remains in the executable helper and installer paths: the skill runs shell commands, reads plaintext Bitbucket credentials, writes pipeline logs, installs dependencies, and can trigger or stop pipelines. I found no evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, but the command construction issues should be fixed before marketplace publication.

high_findings.title: Shell Command Injection Risk in Helper Wrapper
high_findings.description: The helper builds a single shell command string and executes it with execSync. Repository, workspace, branch, pipeline UUID, and other arguments are interpolated into that string without validation or execFile-style argument separation, so a crafted value could alter the shell command.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes interpolated argument strings to execSync. The inputs are expected Bitbucket identifiers, but the helper does not enforce that constraint before shell execution.

high_findings.title: Credentials Embedded in Git Shell Commands
high_findings.description: The helper reads Bitbucket credentials from local files, embeds the password into an HTTPS Git URL, and passes that URL to git commands through a shell string. This can expose secrets through process inspection or command logging and also compounds command injection risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential loading and authenticated URL construction are explicit. Git commands are then run with that URL in the command line, which is a well-known secret exposure pattern.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext Credential Files Are Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to store Bitbucket app passwords in credentials.json or .bitbucket-credentials files. This is functional but raises secret handling risk if files are committed, synced, or readable by other local processes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly document and create plaintext credential storage. This is expected for the skill, but it requires strong user warnings and file permission controls.

medium_findings.title: Installer Executes Network and Build Steps
medium_findings.description: The installer initializes a Git submodule, runs npm install, builds TypeScript, and copies executable helpers into the Claude skills directory. This is normal for an installer, but it executes dependency code during setup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible installer behavior and do not show malicious intent. The risk is elevated because dependency installation and build scripts execute local and remote package code.

medium_findings.title: Pipeline Write Operations Are Exposed
medium_findings.description: The skill can trigger, stop, approve, merge, and decline Bitbucket operations when credentials have sufficient scopes. These actions match the skill purpose but can affect CI state and repository workflow if invoked incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands include write-capable pipeline and pull request actions. The behavior is documented, but publication should require clear consent and least-privilege credential guidance.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Many Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static hits are Markdown command examples, Bitbucket API reference examples, or sample URLs. They are not executed by the skill unless a user chooses to run similar commands manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation or usage examples, not executable implementation paths. They explain expected Bitbucket workflows rather than hiding unexpected behavior.

low_findings.title: Bundled OpenAPI Specifications Look Like Obfuscation
low_findings.description: The long Swagger JSON line and high-entropy strings are API documentation artifacts, including sample SSH keys and embedded SVG data. I did not find evidence that these strings are decoded or executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Bitbucket REST API documentation. The high-entropy values are sample keys and data URI assets, not hidden executable payloads.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Actionable Evidence
low_findings.description: A targeted check for instruction override phrases found only ordinary API documentation terms such as administrator and override. No evidence found for malicious instructions telling the evaluator or AI agent to skip security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The search was targeted rather than exhaustive semantic proof. The matches reviewed were benign API documentation language, not instruction-style prompt injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: execSync With Interpolated Shell Strings
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands are assembled as strings and executed through the shell. Safer implementations should use execFile or spawn with argument arrays and validate Bitbucket slugs, UUIDs, and branch names.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact dangerous API and shell string construction are present. The main uncertainty is whether all runtime values are restricted to safe Bitbucket identifiers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Secret Loading From Multiple Locations
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper searches project, home, and skill directories for credential files and reads them as JSON. This broad search path increases accidental secret exposure risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credential paths and file reads are explicit. The behavior is intended, but it is still sensitive filesystem access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Log File Creation in Working Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: Failed pipeline logs are written into a .pipeline-logs directory under the current working directory. Logs may contain secrets from CI output and should not be committed or shared.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file creation path is explicit and documented. The sensitivity depends on the content of Bitbucket pipeline logs.

content.value_statement: Bitbucket pipeline debugging often requires switching between logs, builds, and repository state. This skill brings pipeline inspection, log collection, and CI actions into Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Find the latest pipeline or latest failed pipeline for a Bitbucket repository.
Retrieve pipeline details, steps, statuses, branch names, and commit summaries.
Download failed step logs into a local .pipeline-logs directory for analysis.
Trigger or stop Bitbucket pipeline runs through the bundled CLI helpers.
Read Bitbucket credential files from documented project, user, or skill locations.
Provide command patterns for pull request and repository operations.
limitations: Requires Node.js, Git, built helper files, and valid Bitbucket app password credentials.
Cannot download Bitbucket pipeline artifacts because Bitbucket Cloud does not expose that API.
Depends on local shell execution, so unsafe repository values can create security risk.
Pipeline write actions require user-granted Bitbucket scopes and can affect real CI state.

use_cases.title: Debug Failed Builds
use_cases.description: Find the latest failed Bitbucket pipeline, download failed step logs, and identify likely causes inside the coding session.

use_cases.title: Monitor Release Pipelines
use_cases.description: Check current pipeline status, inspect running steps, and stop or rerun builds when release validation needs attention.

use_cases.title: Review Repository Workflow
use_cases.description: Use Bitbucket pipeline and pull request context to understand failures, approvals, and merge readiness before a deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Check Latest Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the latest Bitbucket pipeline for this repository. Tell me the status, branch, commit, and build number.

prompt_templates.title: Find Failed Build Logs
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the latest failed Bitbucket pipeline, download the failed step logs, and summarize the first errors I should fix.

prompt_templates.title: Monitor a Running Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Monitor the current Bitbucket pipeline until it finishes. Report running steps, completed steps, and any failure signals.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Debug Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose the failing Bitbucket pipeline, propose a minimal fix, and trigger a new pipeline after I approve the change.

output_examples.input: What is the latest failed pipeline in this repository?
output_examples.output: The latest failed pipeline is build 123 on main. The deploy step failed after eight minutes. The primary error is a missing environment variable.

output_examples.input: Download logs for pipeline 123 and summarize them.
output_examples.output: Two failed step logs were saved locally. The test step failed first, and the deploy step failed because the test artifact was missing.

output_examples.input: Trigger the staging pipeline for this branch.
output_examples.output: The staging pipeline was started for the selected branch. I will monitor status and report failures before suggesting fixes.

best_practices: Use a Bitbucket app password with the minimum repository and pipeline scopes needed.
Confirm workspace, repository, branch, and pipeline identifiers before running write actions.
Add .pipeline-logs and credential files to .gitignore before downloading logs or storing secrets.
anti_patterns: Do not use production credentials when read-only pipeline inspection is enough.
Do not run pipeline write actions without confirming the target branch and repository.
Do not paste untrusted workspace, repository, branch, or UUID values into helper commands.

faq.question: Does this skill require Bitbucket credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. It needs a Bitbucket app password stored in a supported credential file before API calls can work.

faq.question: Can it download pipeline artifacts?
faq.answer: No. The included documentation states that Bitbucket Cloud does not provide an artifact download API.

faq.question: Can it change pipeline state?
faq.answer: Yes. With sufficient scopes, it can trigger and stop pipelines through the bundled CLI helpers.

faq.question: Where are failed logs stored?
faq.answer: Failed step logs are written to a local .pipeline-logs directory in the current working directory.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Not yet. The helper should replace shell string execution with argument-array execution and stricter input validation.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
